perturbation.original_text: An “affiliate” is any legal entity that one of us owns, that owns one of us or that is under common control with one of us. “Control” and “own” mean possessing a 50% or greater interest in entity or the right to direct the management of the entity.
perturbation.changed_text: An “affiliate” is any legal entity that one of us owns, that owns one of us or that is under common control with one of us. “Control” and “own” mean possessing a 25% or greater interest in entity or the right to direct the management of the entity.
perturbation.explanation: The definition of 'control' is altered to a 25% interest, conflicting with standard legal definitions requiring a higher threshold for control.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text's definition of 'control' (25% ownership) contradicts the SEC's interpretation, which typically implies a higher degree of influence or authority.  The SEC often looks beyond mere ownership percentages to determine control, but the contract's definition would be considered a lower bar than what the SEC considers in their investigations.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 240.12b-2 - Definitions. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.12b-2 - Definitions. CFR Table of Popular Names prev | next § 240.12b-2 Definitions. Unless the context otherwise requires, the following terms, when used in the rules contained in this regulation or in Regulation 13A or 15D or in the forms for statements and reports filed pursuant to sections 12, 13 or 15(d) of the act, shall have the respective meanings indicated in this rule: Accelerated filer and large accelerated filer —(1) Accelerated filer. The term accelerated filer means an issuer after it first meets the following conditions as of the end of its fiscal year: (i) The issuer had an aggregate worldwide market value of the voting and non-voting common equity held by its non-affiliates of $75 million or more, but less than $700 million, as of the last business day of the issuer 's most recently completed second fiscal quarter; (ii) The issuer has been subject to the requirements of section 13(a) or 15(d) of the Act ( 15 U.S.C. 78m or 78o(d) ) for a period of at least twelve calendar months; and (iii) The issuer has filed at least one annual report pursuant to section 13(a) or 15(d) of the Act; and (iv) The issuer is not eligible to use the requirements for smaller reporting companies under the revenue test in paragraph (2) or (3)(iii)(B) of the “smaller reporting company” definition in this section , as applicable. (2) Large accelerated filer. The term large accelerated filer means an issuer after it first meets the following conditions as of the end of its fiscal year: (i) The issuer had an aggregate worldwide market value of the voting and non-voting common equity held by its non-affiliates of $700 million or more, as of the last business day of the issuer 's most recently completed second fiscal quarter; (ii) The issuer has been subject to the requirements of section 13(a) o
perturbation.justification: The changed text alters the definition of 'control' to mean possessing a 25% or greater interest, which contradicts the general understanding and potential SEC scrutiny related to control, as suggested in the Law Explanation. While the snippets don't explicitly define 'control', the Law Explanation mentions that the SEC's interpretation typically implies a higher degree of influence or authority than a mere 25% ownership, making the contract's definition a lower bar than what the SEC considers. This misalignment with potential regulatory interpretation establishes a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Confidential Information” is non-public information, know-how and trade secrets in any form that: • Are designated as “confidential”; or • A reasonable person knows or reasonably should understand to be confidential.
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Information” is non-public information, know-how and trade secrets in any form that: • Are designated as “confidential”; or • Any person knows or reasonably should understand to be confidential.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "A reasonable person" to "Any person" broadens the scope of what is considered confidential, potentially conflicting with the trade secret legal standard, which emphasizes objective reasonableness.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UTSA defines misappropriation partly based on what a person *knows or has reason to know*. Changing the 'reasonable person' standard to 'any person' deviates from established trade secret law that requires objective reasonableness in determining whether information should be considered confidential. A trade secret must derive independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not being readily ascertainable by proper means by, other persons who can obtain economic value from its disclosure or use.

perturbation.justification: The change from 'a reasonable person' to 'any person' significantly broadens the scope of what is considered confidential information. The Law Explanation notes this deviates from the established legal standard requiring 'objective reasonableness'. This deviation creates a legal contradiction because trade secret law emphasizes an objective standard when determining if something should be considered confidential. While the provided snippets are unhelpful, the Law Explanation makes clear how this change undermines trade secret law, and the Perturbation Explanation further emphasizes the contradiction by explicitly saying the change 'potentially conflicting with the trade secret legal standard, which emphasizes objective reasonableness.'

perturbation.original_text: The laws of the State of Washington govern this agreement. If federal jurisdiction exists, we each consent to exclusive jurisdiction and venue in the federal courts in King County, Washington. If not, we each consent to exclusive jurisdiction and venue in the superior court of King county, Washington.
perturbation.changed_text: The laws of the State of Delaware govern this agreement. If federal jurisdiction exists, we each consent to exclusive jurisdiction and venue in the federal courts in King County, Washington. If not, we each consent to exclusive jurisdiction and venue in the superior court of King county, Washington.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law from Washington to Delaware creates potential conflicts if the company has no connection to Delaware, raising questions about 'forum shopping' and the enforceability of the clause.
perturbation.law_explanation: While parties can agree to a forum, 28 U.S.C. § 1391 dictates the proper venue for federal cases. Choosing Delaware law when the relevant activities occur in Washington State might contradict principles of venue based on where the defendants reside or where the cause of action arose, especially if the case is brought in Washington.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 28 U.S. Code § 1391 - Venue generally | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 28 U.S. Code § 1391 - Venue generally U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) Applicability of Section.— Except as otherwise provided by law— (1) this section shall govern the venue of all civil actions brought in district courts of the United States; and (2) the proper venue for a civil action shall be determined without regard to whether the action is local or transitory in nature. (b) Venue in General.— A civil action may be brought in— (1) a judicial district in which any defendant resides, if all defendants are residents of the State in which the district is located; (2) a judicial district in which a substantial part of the events or omissions giving rise to the claim occurred, or a substantial part of property that is the subject of the action is situated; or (3) if there is no district in which an action may otherwise be brought as provided in this section, any judicial district in which any defendant is subject to the court’s personal jurisdiction with respect to such action. (c) Residency.— For all venue purposes— (1) a natural person, including an alien lawfully admitted for permanent residence in the United States, shall be deemed to reside in the judicial district in which that person is domiciled; (2) an entity with the capacity to sue and be sued in its common name under applicable law, whether or not incorporated, shall be deemed to reside, if a defendant, in any judicial district in which such defendant is subject to the court’s personal jurisdiction with respect to the civil action in question and, if a plaintiff, only in the judicial district in which it maintains its principal place of business; and (3) a defendant not resident in the United States may be sued in any judicial district, and the joinder of such a defendant shall be disregarded in determining where the action may be brought w
perturbation.justification: The modified text changes the governing law from Washington to Delaware. The Perturbation Explanation states that this raises concerns about 'forum shopping' and the enforceability if the company lacks connection to Delaware. The Law Explanation suggests a contradiction with 28 U.S.C. § 1391, which governs venue. Scraped Law Snippet 2, referencing 28 U.S. Code § 1391, states that a civil action may be brought in a judicial district where the defendant resides or where a substantial part of the events occurred. Choosing Delaware law could contradict venue principles if the relevant activities occurred in Washington.

perturbation.original_text: Either of us may terminate this agreement for any reason by providing the other with 30 days’ advance written notice.
perturbation.changed_text: Either of us may terminate this agreement for any reason by providing the other with 5 days’ advance written notice.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the termination notice period from 30 days to 5 days may be deemed unreasonable, particularly if significant confidential information is involved. This contradicts general legal principles regarding reasonable notice.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 204 addresses situations where a contract is silent or unclear on a specific term. Courts often imply a 'reasonable' term. Reducing the notice period to 5 days could be deemed unreasonable by a court, especially if one party is significantly disadvantaged by the short notice. What constitutes 'reasonable' depends on the nature of the contract and the potential harm caused by termination, and 5 days is very short for a contract with sensitive information.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-204. Formation in General. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-204. Formation in General. (1) A contract for sale of goods may be made in any manner sufficient to show agreement , including conduct by both parties which recognizes the existence of such a contract . (2) An agreement sufficient to constitute a contract for sale may be found even though the moment of its making is undetermined. (3) Even though one or more terms are left open a contract for sale does not fail for indefiniteness if the parties have intended to make a contract and there is a reasonably certain basis for giving an appropriate remedy. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes

perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies a 30-day notice period for termination, while the changed contract reduces this to 5 days. The Law Explanation suggests that reducing the notice period to 5 days could be deemed unreasonable, especially if one party is significantly disadvantaged or if the contract involves sensitive information. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is about contract formation in general, and Scraped Law Snippet 2 is irrelevant, the Law Explanation, combined with the Perturbation Explanation, indicates a potential contradiction with the implied legal requirement of 'reasonable' notice. The reduction from 30 days to 5 days directly undermines the principle of reasonable notice, creating a legal contradiction.
